feature_name: Dual-mode operation: Root Complex (RC) and End Point (EP)
description: The PCIe core supports operation as either a Root Complex or an End Point selectable by bootstrap pins and software. In EP mode it supports legacy and native endpoint behavior for compatibility. In RC mode it behaves as a downstream port that enumerates and configures EPs. Software can override the bootstrapped mode via device-level registers to switch roles. LTSSM must be enabled after configuration to begin link negotiation. Mode changes require reinitialization and link retraining.
dependencies: Power domain on; SerDes PLL lock; DEVSTAT[PCIESSMODE] set
Remarks: Select RC or EP during early initialization; do not toggle at run-time without full reset and retrain.

feature_name: Link capabilities: x1/x2 lanes, Gen1 2.5 GT/s and Gen2 5.0 GT/s
description: A single bidirectional link operates with up to two lanes (×1/×2). The core supports 2.5 or 5.0 Gbps per lane with 8b/10b encoding for 2.0/4.0 Gbps effective throughput. Dynamic width conversion allows Gen1/Gen2 transitions while keeping a constant PHY PLL rate. Automatic lane reversal and RX polarity inversion simplify board routing. Ultra-low TX/RX latency is targeted by the architecture. Negotiated speed and width are readable in link status registers.
dependencies: SerDes configured; Common-clock indication as needed; PL_GEN2[DIR_SPD] used for Gen2 switch
Remarks: Verify LINK_STAT_CTRL[LINK_SPEED,NEGOTIATED_LINK_WD] after training; set PL_GEN2.DIR_SPD to upgrade speed post Gen1 lock.

description: The module supports standard Memory, Configuration (Type0/1), and Message TLPs per PCIe. Posted writes have no completion while non-posted reads and writes receive completions. Locked Memory Read and its locked completion are not supported by the core. Inbound I/O read/write transactions are also not supported. Software must avoid issuing unsupported TLPs to prevent UR or undefined behavior.
dependencies: N/A
Remarks: Disallow inbound I/O and locked read usages in drivers; rely on memory/config/message transactions only.

description: Outbound translation maps internal bus addresses to PCIe addresses for memory TLPs. The PCIESS internal space is partitioned into 32 equal regions of 1/2/4/8 MB using OB_SIZE for flexible tiling. Each region uses OB_OFFSET_INDEXn/OB_OFFSETn_HI to form 32- or 64‑bit PCIe addresses. Bursts that cross a translation region boundary have unspecified behavior and must be avoided. Software should program regions before enabling outbound traffic and size regions to fit DMA bursts. Region index extraction depends on OB_SIZE and must match the internal address map.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]=1; Proper region size/indexing
Remarks: Keep each DMA burst within a single region; use multiple regions to tile large windows cleanly.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions associated to BARs)
description: Inbound translation remaps accepted PCIe memory accesses to internal addresses. Four regions (0–3) are defined and tied to BAR matches via IB_BARn and IB_START/IB_OFFSET registers. BAR0 is reserved for application/config registers and cannot be remapped, ensuring register reachability. An option allows using BAR1 value as a start address when IB_START is zero for a relative mapping. 32‑bit and 64‑bit addressing are supported and must be configured consistently with BARs.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]=1; BARs programmed; Link up
Remarks: Ensure non-overlapping inbound regions; remember BAR0 always lands on application/config space for both RC and EP.

feature_name: BAR Mask registers for EP pre-enumeration sizing
description: BAR mask registers overlay BARs to define aperture size, type, and enable state prior to RC enumeration. They are writable only when CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2] is set and are write-only (reads show BARs). EP firmware can advertise desired window sizes that RC will read during enumeration for resource assignment. BAR0 mask is constrained because BAR0 maps application registers. After setup, clear DBI_CS2 and validate mask effect using test writes before handing control to RC.
dependencies: DBI_CS2=1 during programming; BAR enabled; DBI_CS2 cleared before enumeration
Remarks: Do not attempt to change masks over PCIe; configure locally before link-up for deterministic enumeration behavior.

description: The PCIESS supports system big‑endian operation via configurable byte‑lane swapping. The ENDIAN register selects swap on 1/2/4/8‑byte boundaries for both directions to match the system endianness. Little‑endian systems require no swapping and can leave ENDIAN at default. Byte‑enable reordering follows the defined tables for inbound and outbound paths to ensure data correctness. MMR and non‑prefetchable accesses use 32‑bit boundary behavior on outbound. Validate with known patterns to prevent silent corruption.
dependencies: Device big‑endian boot configuration if used
Remarks: Pick one mode system‑wide and verify with known patterns to avoid silent data corruption across endpoints.

feature_name: VBUSM master/slave ports and DMA usage
description: A 128‑bit VBUSM slave port accepts outbound requests from local EDMA/CPU to the PCIe link. A 128‑bit VBUSM master port issues inbound accesses into internal memory on behalf of remote bus masters. No internal DMA is present; use system EDMA or CPU to generate bursts and manage alignment. Effective throughput is ~85% of the 2.0/4.0 Gbps per lane after 8b/10b overhead and protocol effects. Both ports are available in RC and EP modes for symmetric data movement. Plan interconnect QoS accordingly.
dependencies: EDMA configured; Interconnect bandwidth/QoS sufficient
Remarks: Align EDMA aCnt/bCnt with Max Payload; avoid non‑incrementing bursts; provision fabric QoS for sustained transfers.

description: Up to 14 interrupt events are exposed to the SoC interrupt controller. These include legacy INTA–D reception (RC), MSI groups (0–31) aggregated into eight sets, error classes, and power/reset events. Each class has raw, enable, and enabled‑status registers for masking and servicing. End‑of‑interrupt requires writing the event number to IRQ_EOI after clearing status to re‑arm. Event availability and semantics depend on RC versus EP role and must be handled in the ISR.
evidence: Section 2.14; Table 2‑10; IRQ_EOI; MSIn_IRQ_*; LEGACY_*; ERR_*; PMRST_*
dependencies: Interrupt routing to CPU; Link operational for PCIe‑sourced events
Remarks: Service ordering: clear status first, then write IRQ_EOI; handle legacy race where deassert may arrive after EOI.

description: MSI is signaled by a memory write to a configured address/data pair specific to the vector. EP generates MSI by writing the MSI_IRQ vector in RC (or another EP’s BAR0 on this IP) after enabling MSI. RC and EP implement MSIn enable/status/clear registers for 32 vectors grouped by fours for efficient handling. Capability registers program address, data, and vector count, and INTx must be disabled when MSI is enabled. MSI and legacy interrupts are mutually exclusive per function.
dependencies: MSI_CAP[MSI_EN]=1; INTx disabled; MSI address/data valid; BAR0 reachable
Remarks: Batch ISR handling by group; always clear MSIn_IRQ_STATUS then write IRQ_EOI to re‑arm events.

feature_name: Legacy interrupts: RC reception and EP generation
description: RC receives INTA–INTD messages and must probe downstream configuration space to find the source. EP asserts legacy via EP_IRQ_SET and deasserts via EP_IRQ_CLR, with one pending at a time reflected in EP_IRQ_STATUS. Due to posted writes and relaxed ordering, races can occur between data and interrupt delivery, so drivers should ensure ordering. EOI handling must consider deassert timing relative to EOI to avoid retriggering. Design drivers to ensure data completion before interrupt signaling to the RC.
dependencies: Legacy capability configured; Interrupt controller mapping
Remarks: Disable relaxed ordering for control paths if strict ordering is needed; probe multiple EPs before clearing RC INTx.

feature_name: Power management: D‑states and ASPM L0s/L1
description: Device power states D0/D1/D2/D3hot/D3cold are supported per PCIe with D3cold lacking Vaux retention. Link power states L0/L0s/L1 are supported, while L2 is not, and L3 is power‑off. ASPM can autonomously transition to L0s/L1 when enabled, and software can couple link state to device D‑states. PMCAP and PM_CTL_STAT configure PME, state transitions, and reporting as required by platform policy. Device‑link state combinations are constrained as defined to maintain compliance.
dependencies: Platform policy; RC cooperation for ASPM; PME routing
Remarks: Ensure clocks/power are valid on D‑state exit; disable ASPM during bring‑up until stability is verified; L2 is not supported.

description: AER implements detailed error status, masking, and severity classification for correctable and uncorrectable errors. Uncorrectable errors are tracked in PCIE_UNCERR with masks and severities in PCIE_UNCERR_MASK/SVRTY to tailor handling. Correctable errors are reported via PCIE_CERR and masked by PCIE_CERR_MASK to avoid storms. Root ports use ROOT_ERR_CMD/ROOT_ERR_ST and ERR_SRC_ID to route, enable, and identify errors end‑to‑end. Drivers should enable only manageable classes and log header registers HDR_LOG0–3 for triage.
dependencies: RC software for error routing; Interrupt wiring for AER
Remarks: Set severity to match system policy; mask chatty correctable sources; capture header logs on first detected error.

description: End‑to‑End CRC can be enabled to protect TLP payload integrity on capable links. PCIE_ACCR provides ECRC generation and check enable bits plus capability indicators that reflect hardware support. Some link partners may not support ECRC, especially when the device operates as RC. The guide recommends disabling ECRC when peers lack support to avoid false positives and interop issues. The first‑error pointer assists in identifying the original faulting condition for debugging.
dependencies: Peer device capability; Coordinated AER handling
Remarks: Enable ECRC only with known‑good peers; otherwise clear ECRC_CHK_EN/GEN_EN to prevent interoperability issues.

feature_name: PCIe loopback: PIPE and PHY modes
description: PIPE loopback supports symbol‑level testing between loopback master and slave endpoints with compliance tools. Either RC or EP can be loopback master/slave in PIPE mode, but TLPs are not looped back through the fabric. PHY loopback loops TX to RX inside the PHY and can be used without a link partner, but setup is RC‑only. Configuration requires SERDES_CFGx loopback, forcing LTSSM via PL_FORCE_LINK, and link training steps as documented. Set loopback before traffic to avoid sequence number mismatches on receivers.
dependencies: SerDes configured; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN] sequencing
Remarks: Ensure BAR mapping does not capture looped traffic; skip Detect by forcing POLL_ACTIVE during PHY loopback as documented.

description: Hardware provides power‑on and system (hard/soft) resets, and software can issue a downstream hot reset. RSTCMD[INIT_RST] triggers a downstream hot reset, and FLUSH_N indicates pending traffic before asserting resets. Soft reset preserves sticky bits and PM logic; full reset clears the subsystem per register defaults. After any reset, drivers must delay before issuing PCIe transactions to respect spec timing. Power domain cycling resets the module and requires re‑initialization and link retraining.
dependencies: PSC/domain control; Reset sequencing
Remarks: Observe ≥100 ms delay after reset before accesses; ensure traffic is quiesced on both ends before power cycling PCIe domain.

description: Bring‑up sequences define ordered steps for RC and EP roles to ensure deterministic startup. They include enabling the SerDes PLL, powering the module, programming configuration and BARs, and enabling LTSSM. RC additionally performs enumeration, sets Base/Limit windows, and configures downstream devices via config cycles. EP can use APP_RETRY_EN to respond with retries while local initialization proceeds. Completion occurs after link training reaches L0 and software handshakes are finished.
dependencies: Stable refclk; SerDes lock; Power domain on
Remarks: Follow documented order to avoid deadlocks; clear APP_RETRY_EN once EP is ready to respond to RC configuration.

description: The PHY requires a clean differential refclock and internal PLL configuration via PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL. Software enables ENPLL, waits for stabilization, and confirms LOCK in PCIE_SERDES_STS before any PCIESS access. Loop bandwidth (LB) and MPY multiplier must be set to match refclock and target line rate, with RATESCALE fixed by device. A common‑clock architecture improves training behavior and SSC tolerance and must be reflected in COMMON_CLK_CFG. Registers are in the chip configuration domain and require kicker unlock per device manual when applicable.
dependencies: Reference clock present; Proper LB/MPY; PSC clocks enabled
Remarks: Program PLL then poll LOCK before touching PCIESS registers; set COMMON_CLK_CFG if refclks are shared to reduce training time.

feature_name: Address Space 0 layout and access rules
description: Address Space 0 is a 16‑KB window subdivided into 4‑KB regions for application registers, local configuration, remote configuration, and remote I/O (RC only). These regions only allow single 32‑bit transactions and must be marked non‑cacheable in the CPU MMU. Remote configuration accesses require setting CFG_SETUP to target bus/device/function prior to the access. Accesses to remote windows without an operational link can hang the initiator or timeout downstream. Drivers must serialize such transactions and guard them with link‑state checks.
dependencies: Link status checks; Correct mapping of Address Space 0 base
Remarks: Wrap remote transactions with link‑up checks; never burst in these windows; ensure 32‑bit aligned single accesses only.

description: Port Logic and PCIe Capability registers control link enable, resets, loopback, speed changes, and ASPM. PL_LINK_CTRL enables DLL/link, asserts hot reset, controls scrambling, and emits vendor messages as needed. LINK_STAT_CTRL exposes negotiated speed/width and common‑clock status and controls ASPM entries for power saving. PL_GEN2 provides directed speed change and TX swing/de‑emphasis settings for Gen2 operation. These registers are central during bring‑up, recovery, and compliance testing workflows.
dependencies: SerDes ready; Correct sequencing with LTSSM
Remarks: Apply changes with awareness of LTSSM state; read back negotiated fields to confirm effects; avoid disabling link under load.

description: DEBUG0/DEBUG1 expose LTSSM state, training sequence reception, PIPE data, and electrical‑idle indications. DIAG_CTRL can inject single‑packet LCRC/ECRC inversions to validate error handling paths end‑to‑end. ACT_STATUS reports whether inbound and outbound buffers contain data for safe quiesce prior to reset or power actions. These facilities accelerate link bring‑up and failure analysis during validation. Use with caution on production links to avoid unintended error injection or service impact.
dependencies: N/A
Remarks: Log LTSSM encodings per Appendix A; reset DIAG_CTRL after use; poll ACT_STATUS to ensure quiescence before resets.

feature_name: TLP and completion filtering/UR policy controls
description: Filter/mask registers allow fine control of which TLPs are accepted and how UR conditions are reported. SYMTIMER_FLTMASK can allow or block CFG/IO/MSG TLPs on RC and adjust UR mapping rules for development. It also controls completion attribute/length/function matching and Flow Control watchdog behavior. FLT_MASK2 controls DLLP abort and vendor message drop behavior to tailor link behavior. These knobs are useful for validation but must be used judiciously in production to maintain compliance.
dependencies: System policy; Thorough validation
Remarks: Keep defaults unless a test requires change; misconfiguration can mask protocol violations or cause interoperability issues.

feature_name: Performance knobs: payloads, MRRS, ACK/TS timers
description: Maximum payload size and maximum read request size are configured in DEV_STAT_CTRL and DEVICE_CAP fields. ACK/Nak frequency and FT sequences are tunable via ACK_FREQ and SYM_NUM to balance latency and bandwidth. These parameters affect throughput, latency, and interoperability margins across a PCIe fabric. Conservative settings ease bring‑up; tuned settings increase throughput once stability is proven. Always coordinate settings at both ends where applicable to avoid asymmetry issues.
dependencies: Peer device capabilities; Stable link
Remarks: Start with smaller MRRS/payload then raise; ensure ACK_FREQ!=0 only when tested; document tuned values for certification.

feature_name: RC configuration and I/O access windows
description: RC performs configuration cycles by programming CFG_SETUP and then accessing the local config window. A 4‑KB I/O window exists for outbound I/O transactions with base supplied by IOBASE, which the RC converts to IO TLPs. Inbound I/O is not supported and must not be relied upon anywhere in the system. Drivers should serialize config writes and guard I/O window usage to avoid bus faults and non‑responsive targets. Misuse can lead to timeouts or UR responses that disturb initialization flows.
dependencies: RC mode only; Link up; Proper BDF targeting
Remarks: Validate BDF before config cycles; avoid bursts; implement timeout/retry policy around I/O transactions.

description: The design does not support using a ×2 link as two ×1 links or multiple virtual channels or traffic classes. There is no function‑level reset, vendor messaging, hot‑plug controller, or L2 link state support in this IP. Inbound I/O is unsupported in both RC and EP modes and must be avoided in software. There is no auxiliary power retention for D3cold to D0 restoration paths. System architects must plan for these limitations and provide external mechanisms where required.
dependencies: N/A
Remarks: Plan for external hot‑plug management if required; keep link in L0/L0s/L1 only; avoid L2 power expectations and FLR usage.
